title: Hybrid Search with RRF: Fusing Dense and Sparse
seoTitle: Hybrid Search & RRF: Fusing Dense and Sparse Results
metaDescription: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines dense and sparse retrieval into one ranked list. Learn how RRF fuses results and why it is the default hybrid search pattern.
oneLiner: You will understand how hybrid search runs dense and sparse retrieval in parallel and fuses their ranked lists with Reciprocal Rank Fusion to get one better ranking.

blocks.md: Modern [RAG](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag) systems usually search your documents two different ways at once. One way is **keyword** search ([BM25](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-bm25)) — it matches the exact words in the question, so it nails error codes, product names, and rare acronyms. The other is **vector** search — it matches on *meaning*, so it finds a passage about "reimbursing a customer" even when the question says "refund." Each is strong exactly where the other is weak.

That leaves a problem: now you have **two ranked lists** of results, and they disagree. Keyword search ranks document A first; vector search ranks document C first. You can only paste a handful of chunks into the prompt, so which list wins? **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** is the simple, boring, reliable answer. It merges the two lists into one combined ranking, letting a result that *either* method liked float to the top.

Here is the everyday version. Imagine two friends recommend restaurants. One ranks by *cuisine you'd love*, the other by *places nearby*. You don't try to convert their gut feelings into the same 0-to-100 score — that would be hopeless. Instead you just look at *where each place sits on each friend's list*. A restaurant that's near the top of **both** lists is almost certainly your best bet. RRF formalizes exactly that instinct: it scores each result by its *rank position* on every list, then adds those up.

RRF is a **rank fusion** method: it ignores the raw similarity scores and uses only the *position* (1st, 2nd, 3rd…) a result holds in each list. That one design choice is why it works so well across mismatched retrievers — more on that below. This article is the hands-on fusion-mechanics companion to the conceptual [hybrid search](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-hybrid-search) and [RRF](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/reciprocal-rank-fusion) explainers.

The hard part of combining two retrievers is not running them — it's *comparing their scores*. A BM25 score might be `14.7`; a cosine similarity is `0.83`. They live on totally different scales, mean totally different things, and shift from query to query. If you naively add or average them, the method with the bigger raw numbers silently dominates, and your "hybrid" search is really just keyword search wearing a costume.

RRF sidesteps the whole mess. By throwing away the raw scores and keeping only the rank, it never has to make a BM25 score and a vector score "agree." This buys you three concrete things a builder cares about:

items: **No score calibration.** You don't normalize, you don't tune weights to balance two scales, you don't re-tune them every time you swap your embedding model. Rank is rank regardless of the retriever.
**Robust to outliers.** One retriever returning a wildly large score for a junk result can't blow up the fused ranking, because only its *position* counts — and one bad position is easily outvoted.

**Trivial to extend.** Adding a third retriever (say, a [learned sparse](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/cross-encoder-vs-bi-encoder) model or a metadata-filtered list) is just one more list to fuse. The formula doesn't change.

blocks.md: This is why RRF is the **consensus production default** for hybrid retrieval. Search engines and vector databases — Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Weaviate, Qdrant, and others — ship RRF as the built-in way to combine a keyword query and a vector query. When a tutorial says "just turn on hybrid search," the fusion happening under the hood is almost always RRF. It is the unglamorous default precisely because it rarely needs babysitting.

Hybrid search with RRF is a three-move pipeline: **retrieve in parallel**, **fuse the ranks**, **take the top results**. The two retrievers don't know about each other; they each return their own ranked list, and RRF stitches those lists together afterward.

For each document, RRF adds up a small score from every list it appears in. A document at rank `r` in a list contributes `1 / (k + r)`, where `k` is a constant (a value of `60` is the long-standing default from the original paper). Sum those contributions across all lists and sort by the total. That's the entire algorithm.

blocks.title: RRF score for one document
blocks.code:
RRF_score(doc) = sum over each list L of:  1 / (k + rank_of_doc_in_L)

with k = 60 (the conventional default)

blocks.md: Three properties fall out of that tiny formula, and they're the reason it behaves so well:

items: **Top ranks matter most.** `1/(60+1) ≈ 0.0164` for rank 1, but only `1/(60+10) ≈ 0.0143` for rank 10. The contribution shrinks as rank grows, so being near the top of a list is worth more — but the curve is *gentle*, not a cliff.

**Appearing on both lists is a big win.** A document ranked, say, 3rd by keyword search and 5th by vector search collects *two* contributions and easily beats a document that only one retriever liked. Agreement across methods is what floats a result up.

**`k` is a smoothing knob.** A larger `k` flattens the difference between ranks (positions matter less, broad agreement matters more); a smaller `k` sharpens the advantage of being rank 1. The default `60` is a deliberately safe middle that you rarely need to touch.

blocks.md: Say a query returns these two short lists. Notice that document **D2** is ranked highly by *both* retrievers — watch it win the fused ranking even though it isn't first on either list.

D1 was *first* on the keyword list and D5 was *second* on the vector list, but D2 — second and first — wins because it scored on **both** sides. That is RRF's core behavior in one table: it rewards cross-method consensus, and it does it without ever comparing a keyword score to a vector score.

Because RRF only needs the ranked lists — not the raw scores — it's almost embarrassingly short to implement yourself. Each retriever hands you an ordered list of document ids; you walk each list, add `1 / (k + rank)` to a running tally per id, and sort.

blocks.title: rrf.py

blocks.md: You usually don't write this by hand in production. Vector databases and search engines expose RRF as a built-in fusion mode on their hybrid query, and frameworks like LangChain offer an ensemble retriever that fuses with RRF. But seeing the fifteen lines makes the magic concrete: there is no calibration, no normalization, no weight tuning — just rank arithmetic.

RRF isn't the only way to combine retrievers. The main alternative is **weighted score fusion**: normalize each retriever's raw scores onto a common 0-to-1 scale, then blend them with a weight (for example `0.5 * vector + 0.5 * keyword`). It's worth knowing when each is the right tool.

diagram.title: Two ways to fuse two retrievers

columns.title: RRF (rank fusion)
items: Uses only rank position
No score normalization needed
Robust to weird/outlier scores
One knob (k), rarely tuned
Great default, set-and-forget

columns.title: Weighted score fusion
items: Uses raw similarity scores
Must normalize the two scales
Outliers can distort the blend
Per-retriever weights to tune
More control if you tune it well

blocks.md: The tradeoff is **simplicity vs. control**. Weighted score fusion *can* beat RRF when you've invested in calibrating scores and tuning the weights for your specific data — because it preserves the *magnitude* of confidence, not just the ordering, so a retriever that's wildly more confident on a query can express that. RRF deliberately throws that information away, which is exactly why it's so robust out of the box. The honest production guidance: **start with RRF**, ship it, and only move to tuned score fusion if evaluation shows you're leaving quality on the table.

Because RRF only sees ranks, it can't tell a *barely* relevant top result from a *strongly* relevant one — a retriever's rank-1 hit always contributes `1/(k+1)` even if that hit is mediocre. That's a feature for robustness and a limitation for precision. The standard fix is a separate [reranking](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-reranking) step *after* fusion, covered below.
RRF is hard to misuse badly, but a few mistakes quietly degrade results. Most of them come from feeding it bad lists rather than from the fusion math itself.

items: **Retrieving too few candidates per list.** If each retriever only returns its top 3, a great document that one method ranked 8th never enters the fusion at all. Retrieve a generous candidate pool from *each* retriever (often 20–100) and fuse those, then trim to the final few.

**Fusing lists of different lengths carelessly.** A retriever that returns a much longer list isn't automatically favored — but make sure every retriever actually ran and returned results. A silently empty list means you fell back to single-method search without noticing.

**Treating `k` as a tuning emergency.** Beginners reach for `k` first. It's the *last* thing to touch. The default 60 is fine for almost everyone; spend your effort on chunking, the candidate pool size, and reranking instead.

**Expecting RRF to fix bad retrievers.** Fusion combines what each retriever found; it can't surface a chunk neither one retrieved. If both your keyword and vector search miss the right passage, RRF will faithfully merge two wrong lists. Quality starts upstream, at [chunking](/learn/rag/rag-fundamentals/what-is-rag) and the retrievers themselves.
**Skipping evaluation.** "Hybrid is better" is true on average, not on *your* data. Measure retrieval quality with and without fusion on a real question set before declaring victory.

blocks.md: Once basic RRF hybrid search is in place, the production pattern almost always grows the same way: **fuse broadly, then rerank narrowly.** A few directions worth knowing.

**RRF then a cross-encoder reranker.** RRF gives you a strong, cheap *recall* layer — it makes sure the right documents are *somewhere* near the top of one merged list. But it can't judge fine-grained relevance because it only sees ranks. So the common stack is: retrieve a big pool from each method, fuse with RRF to maybe 50–100 candidates, then run a [cross-encoder reranker](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/cross-encoder-vs-bi-encoder) that reads the query and each candidate together and re-scores them precisely. Cheap broad fusion, expensive narrow ranking — best of both.

**Fusing more than two lists.** Nothing limits RRF to keyword + vector. You can fuse a [HyDE](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-hyde-rag)-expanded query, several rewritten queries from [query rewriting](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/query-rewriting-for-rag), and a learned-sparse retriever — each produces a ranked list, and RRF sums them all the same way. This makes RRF a natural fusion layer for multi-query retrieval, where one user question fans out into several searches whose results must come back as one list.

**Weighted RRF.** A middle ground between plain RRF and full score fusion is to weight each list's contribution: multiply a list's `1/(k+rank)` terms by a per-retriever weight before summing. You keep RRF's calibration-free robustness but can nudge the fusion toward the retriever you trust more on your domain. Many engines expose this as an optional weight per query. Use it sparingly and only with evaluation to back the weights up.

**The metadata interaction.** Fusion happens *after* retrieval, so any [metadata filtering](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/metadata-filtering-in-rag) (tenant, date, permissions) must be applied to *each* retriever's query, not bolted on afterward — otherwise a filtered-out document can still leak into the fused list from the retriever that didn't filter. The durable lesson with RRF is the same one that runs through all of retrieval: the fusion step is robust and rarely your problem; your effort belongs in the lists you feed it and the [reranker](/learn/rag/retrieval-and-reranking/what-is-reranking) you put after it.

faq.q: What is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) in simple terms?
faq.a: RRF is a method for merging several ranked lists of search results into one combined list. For each document it adds up a small score, `1 / (k + rank)`, from every list the document appears in, then sorts by the total. A document that ranks well in more than one list floats to the top. It uses only rank position, never the raw similarity scores.

faq.q: Why does RRF use rank instead of the actual similarity scores?
faq.a: Because raw scores from different retrievers live on incompatible scales — a BM25 score might be 14.7 while a cosine similarity is 0.83 — and forcing them onto one scale is fragile. Rank position is universal: 1st is 1st regardless of the retriever. Using rank means RRF needs no score normalization and isn't thrown off by outlier scores.

faq.q: What value of k should I use for RRF?
faq.a: The conventional default is k = 60, taken from the original RRF paper, and it works well for almost everyone. A larger k makes rank differences matter less (favoring broad agreement); a smaller k sharpens the advantage of being ranked first. Tune k last — candidate pool size and reranking matter far more.

faq.q: Is RRF the same as hybrid search?
faq.a: Not quite. Hybrid search is the overall idea of combining keyword and vector retrieval; RRF is the specific algorithm most commonly used to *fuse* their two result lists into one. You can do hybrid search with other fusion methods (like weighted score fusion), but RRF is the default in most production tools.

faq.q: When should I use weighted score fusion instead of RRF?
faq.a: Use weighted score fusion when you've already calibrated your retrievers' scores and tuned per-retriever weights for your data, because it preserves how *confident* a retriever is, not just the ordering. For everything else, start with RRF — it's calibration-free and robust out of the box — and only switch if evaluation shows score fusion does better.

faq.q: Do I still need a reranker if I use RRF?
faq.a: Usually yes. RRF is great at recall (getting the right documents near the top of a merged list) but can't judge fine-grained relevance, since it only sees ranks. The standard production stack fuses a broad candidate pool with RRF and then runs a cross-encoder reranker over the top candidates for precise final ordering.
